Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.question: Which body is responsible for issuing International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS)?
options: The International Auditing and Assurance Standards Board (IAASB)
The International Ethics Standards Board for Accountants (IESBA)
The International Public Sector Accounting Standards Board (IPSASB)
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.explanation: The IASB, based in London, develops and publishes IFRS. The IAASB sets auditing standards, the IESBA sets ethics standards, and the IPSASB sets public sector accounting standards.

Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.question: What is the role of the IFRS Foundation?
options: To oversee the IASB and fund its work
To enforce IFRS in member countries
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.correct: To oversee the IASB and fund its work
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.explanation: The IFRS Foundation is the oversight body that appoints the IASB, raises funds and oversees the standard-setting process. The IASB issues IFRS.

Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.question: Who can adopt IFRS for their financial statements?
options: Any entity; adoption is voluntary unless required by local regulation
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.correct: Any entity; adoption is voluntary unless required by local regulation
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.explanation: IFRS can be adopted by any entity. Many jurisdictions require IFRS for listed companies or certain entities; others permit or require local GAAP.

Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.question: What does IAS stand for in the context of international standards?
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.explanation: IAS stands for International Accounting Standard. Many IAS were issued by the former IASC; the IASB has adopted them and issues new IFRS.

Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.question: Which of the following is a primary objective of the IASB?
options: To develop a single set of high-quality global accounting standards
To set ethical codes for accountants
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.correct: To develop a single set of high-quality global accounting standards
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.explanation: The IASB's objective is to develop a single set of high-quality, understandable, enforceable and globally accepted financial reporting standards.

Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.question: When did the IASB replace the IASC?
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.explanation: The IASB replaced the International Accounting Standards Committee (IASC) in 2001. The IASB is part of the IFRS Foundation structure.

Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.question: What is the relationship between IFRS and IAS?
options: IAS are superseded and no longer applicable
IAS adopted by the IASB remain in force unless replaced by IFRS
IFRS apply only to SMEs; IAS apply to listed entities
They are identical in every jurisdiction
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.correct: IAS adopted by the IASB remain in force unless replaced by IFRS
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.explanation: Standards issued by the IASC and adopted by the IASB (IAS) remain in effect unless and until replaced or amended by the IASB (e.g. by an IFRS).

Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.question: Which statement describes the applicability of IFRS to small and medium-sized entities?
options: IFRS do not apply to SMEs
The IASB has issued the IFRS for SMEs Standard for eligible entities
SMEs must use full IFRS only
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.correct: The IASB has issued the IFRS for SMEs Standard for eligible entities
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.explanation: The IASB has issued the IFRS for SMEs Standard, a simplified set of standards for entities that do not have public accountability. Jurisdictions may permit or require its use.

Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.question: What is the purpose of the IFRS Interpretations Committee?
options: To provide timely guidance on applying IFRS and to address emerging issues
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.correct: To provide timely guidance on applying IFRS and to address emerging issues
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.explanation: The IFRS Interpretations Committee (formerly IFRIC) provides guidance on the application of IFRS and addresses issues not explicitly covered in the standards.

Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.question: Which body does the IFRS Foundation trustees appoint?
Chapter 1: International Financial Repor.explanation: The IFRS Foundation trustees appoint the IASB chair and members. The IASB is responsible for developing and issuing IFRS.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: According to the Conceptual Framework, which of the following is a fundamental characteristic that makes financial information useful?
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: The two fundamental qualitative characteristics are relevance and faithful representation. Comparability, verifiability, timeliness and understandability are enhancing characteristics.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: Which of the following is an enhancing qualitative characteristic of useful financial information?
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: Comparability, verifiability, timeliness and understandability are enhancing characteristics. Relevance and faithful representation are fundamental.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: What are the two underlying assumptions in the Conceptual Framework?
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: The Conceptual Framework identifies accrual basis and going concern as the two underlying assumptions on which financial statements are prepared.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: Which of the following is a definition of an asset in the Conceptual Framework?
options: A resource controlled by the entity from which future economic benefits are expected to flow to the entity
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: A resource controlled by the entity from which future economic benefits are expected to flow to the entity
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: An asset is a present economic resource controlled by the entity as a result of past events. An economic resource is a right that has the potential to produce economic benefits.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: What is a liability under the Conceptual Framework?
options: A present obligation of the entity to transfer an economic resource as a result of past events
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: A present obligation of the entity to transfer an economic resource as a result of past events
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: A liability is a present obligation of the entity to transfer an economic resource as a result of past events. Obligations can be legal or constructive.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: When is financial information considered relevant?
options: When it is free from error
When it is capable of making a difference in the decisions made by users
When it is comparable across periods
When it is disclosed in the notes
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: When it is capable of making a difference in the decisions made by users
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: Relevance means the information is capable of making a difference in user decisions. It has predictive value, confirmatory value or both, and is material.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: What does faithful representation require?
options: Information to be complete, neutral and free from material error
Information to be disclosed in the primary statements only
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: Information to be complete, neutral and free from material error
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: Faithful representation requires completeness, neutrality and freedom from material error. It supports the fundamental characteristic of faithful representation.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: Which of the following best describes the going concern assumption?
options: The entity will cease trading within 12 months
The entity is assumed to continue in operation for the foreseeable future
Assets are valued at liquidation value
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: The entity is assumed to continue in operation for the foreseeable future
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: Under the going concern assumption, financial statements are prepared on the basis that the entity will continue in operation for the foreseeable future.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: What is the accrual basis of accounting?
options: Recognising transactions when cash is received or paid
Recognising elements when they meet the definition and recognition criteria, and recognising them when they occur
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.correct: Recognising elements when they meet the definition and recognition criteria, and recognising them when they occur
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: Under the accrual basis, transactions and events are recognised when they occur (and meet the definition and recognition criteria), not when cash is received or paid.

Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.question: Which of the following is not a main element of financial statements in the Conceptual Framework?
Chapter 2: Conceptual Framework.explanation: The main elements related to the statement of financial position are asset, liability and equity. Income and expense relate to performance. Budget is not an element.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: Under IFRS 18, which subtotals are required in the statement of profit or loss?
options: Operating profit and profit before tax only
Operating profit, profit before tax and profit or loss
Gross profit and operating profit only
Profit before tax and profit or loss only
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: Operating profit, profit before tax and profit or loss
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: IFRS 18 requires specified subtotals including operating profit, profit before tax and profit or loss, with defined categories to improve comparability.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: What does IFRS 18 replace in respect of presentation of financial statements?
options: Parts of IAS 1 relating to presentation of the statement of profit or loss
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: Parts of IAS 1 relating to presentation of the statement of profit or loss
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: IFRS 18 replaces the presentation requirements of IAS 1 for the statement of profit or loss (and related guidance), introducing new categories and subtotals.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: Under IFRS 18, how are operating expenses typically categorised?
options: By nature or by function; management chooses
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: By nature or by function; management chooses
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: Entities may present expenses by nature (e.g. depreciation, staff costs) or by function (e.g. cost of sales, distribution). IFRS 18 specifies categories and subtotals.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: What is the purpose of IFRS 18's specified categories in the statement of profit or loss?
options: To improve comparability and structure of the statement
To replace the statement of financial position
To eliminate the need for notes
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: To improve comparability and structure of the statement
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: IFRS 18 introduces defined categories and mandatory subtotals to improve comparability between entities and the usefulness of the statement of profit or loss.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: Under IFRS 18, where is investment income from associates and joint ventures presented?
options: In the specified categories as defined in the standard (e.g. investing category)
As a deduction from cost of sales
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: In the specified categories as defined in the standard (e.g. investing category)
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: IFRS 18 defines categories (e.g. operating, investing, financing) and specifies where items such as share of profit of associates are presented.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: Does IFRS 18 require a single statement of profit or loss or allow a two-statement approach?
options: Two statements only (profit or loss and other comprehensive income)
Either; the standard permits choice where applicable
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: Either; the standard permits choice where applicable
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: IFRS 18 works with the overall structure of financial statements; entities may present one or two statements for profit or loss and OCI as permitted.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: What is the effect of IFRS 18 on the statement of financial position?
options: It replaces the statement of financial position
It does not change the statement of financial position presentation
It removes the requirement for a statement of financial position
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: It does not change the statement of financial position presentation
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: IFRS 18 primarily addresses the statement of profit or loss and related presentation. The statement of financial position is still governed by IAS 1 and other standards.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: Under IFRS 18, how should unusual or infrequent items be presented?
options: As a separate line item outside the specified categories
Within the specified categories; they are not presented as a single line unless material
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: Within the specified categories; they are not presented as a single line unless material
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: Items are included in the appropriate category. Unusual items are not presented as a separate category; they may be disclosed in the notes if material.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: When did IFRS 18 become effective?
options: For periods beginning on or after 1 January 2024
For periods beginning on or after 1 January 2025
For periods beginning on or after 1 January 2027
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: For periods beginning on or after 1 January 2027
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: IFRS 18 is effective for annual periods beginning on or after 1 January 2027, with earlier application permitted.

Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.question: What does IFRS 18 say about management performance measures (e.g. adjusted profit)?
options: They may be presented in the financial statements subject to specified disclosure and reconciliation requirements
They are required in the statement of profit or loss
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.correct: They may be presented in the financial statements subject to specified disclosure and reconciliation requirements
Chapter 3: IFRS 18 Presentation and Disc.explanation: IFRS 18 includes requirements for management-defined performance measures when presented in the financial statements, including reconciliation to GAAP subtotals.

Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.question: When an entity changes an accounting policy, how should it be applied under IAS 8?
options: Prospectively from the date of change only
By restating prior years only in the notes
By disclosing the effect in the current year only
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.explanation: IAS 8 requires retrospective application of a change in accounting policy, with restatement of prior periods, unless retrospective application is impracticable.

Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.question: How should a change in accounting estimate be accounted for under IAS 8?
options: Prospectively in the current and future periods
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.correct: Prospectively in the current and future periods
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.explanation: A change in estimate is applied prospectively from the period of change. It does not require restatement of prior periods.

Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.question: What is a prior period error under IAS 8?
options: An error in opening balance of the current period
An omission or misstatement in prior period financial statements that could reasonably be expected to influence economic decisions
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.correct: An omission or misstatement in prior period financial statements that could reasonably be expected to influence economic decisions
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.explanation: Prior period errors are omissions from, or misstatements in, prior period financial statements arising from a failure to use reliable information that was available.

Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.question: How should material prior period errors be corrected under IAS 8?
options: In the current period profit or loss
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.explanation: Material prior period errors are corrected retrospectively in the first set of financial statements approved after discovery by restating comparative amounts.

Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.question: When may an entity change an accounting policy under IAS 8?
options: Whenever management prefers a different method
Only when required by a standard or interpretation, or when the change results in more reliable and relevant information
Only in the year of adoption of a new IFRS
Never after the first reporting period
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.correct: Only when required by a standard or interpretation, or when the change results in more reliable and relevant information
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.explanation: A change in policy is permitted when required by a standard/interpretation or when it provides reliable and more relevant information.

Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.question: What disclosure does IAS 8 require for a change in accounting policy?
options: The nature of the change, the reason, and the amount of the adjustment for each prior period and the opening balance of the earliest period presented
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.correct: The nature of the change, the reason, and the amount of the adjustment for each prior period and the opening balance of the earliest period presented
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.explanation: IAS 8 requires disclosure of the nature of the change, reason, and for each period presented the amount of the adjustment to each affected line item and to the opening balance of retained earnings (or other component of equity).

Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.question: What is the hierarchy of guidance when no standard applies to a transaction under IAS 8?
options: Management considers the Conceptual Framework, then recent pronouncements of other standard-setters and industry practice
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.correct: Management considers the Conceptual Framework, then recent pronouncements of other standard-setters and industry practice
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.explanation: In making judgement, management refers to the Conceptual Framework, then to recent pronouncements of other standard-setters and accepted industry practice, in that order.

Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.question: Can an entity correct immaterial prior period errors in the current period?
options: No; all errors must be corrected retrospectively
Yes; immaterial errors may be corrected in the current period
Only in the statement of cash flows
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.correct: Yes; immaterial errors may be corrected in the current period
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.explanation: Immaterial prior period errors may be corrected in the current period. Material errors require retrospective restatement.

Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.question: Under IAS 8, what is the difference between a change in accounting policy and a change in accounting estimate?
options: A policy change is applied prospectively; an estimate change retrospectively
A policy change is applied retrospectively (unless impracticable); an estimate change is applied prospectively
Both require restatement of all prior periods
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.correct: A policy change is applied retrospectively (unless impracticable); an estimate change is applied prospectively
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.explanation: Change in policy: retrospective application. Change in estimate: prospective application from the period of change.

Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.question: When restating for a prior period error, what must be restated under IAS 8?
options: Comparative amounts for each prior period presented and the opening balance of the earliest period presented
Only the statement of financial position
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.correct: Comparative amounts for each prior period presented and the opening balance of the earliest period presented
Chapter 4: IAS 8 Basis of Preparation of.explanation: Restatement requires adjusting comparative amounts for each prior period presented and the opening balance of retained earnings (or other equity component) for the earliest period presented.

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.question: Under IFRS 15, when should revenue from a contract with a customer be recognised?
options: When the entity transfers control of the good or service to the customer
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.correct: When the entity transfers control of the good or service to the customer
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.explanation: IFRS 15 requires revenue to be recognised when (or as) the entity transfers control of the promised good or service to the customer.

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.question: What are the five steps in the IFRS 15 revenue recognition model?
options: Identify contract, identify performance obligations, determine price, allocate price, recognise revenue
Identify contract, identify performance obligations, determine transaction price, allocate price to obligations, recognise revenue when (or as) each obligation is satisfied
Estimate revenue, record cash, match costs
Bill customer, collect cash, recognise revenue
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.correct: Identify contract, identify performance obligations, determine transaction price, allocate price to obligations, recognise revenue when (or as) each obligation is satisfied
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.explanation: The five steps are: (1) identify the contract, (2) identify performance obligations, (3) determine transaction price, (4) allocate price to obligations, (5) recognise revenue when/as each obligation is satisfied.

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.question: What is a performance obligation under IFRS 15?
options: A promise in a contract to transfer a good or service to the customer
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.correct: A promise in a contract to transfer a good or service to the customer
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.explanation: A performance obligation is a promise in a contract to transfer a good or service (or a bundle of goods or services) that is distinct, or a series of distinct goods or services that are substantially the same.

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.question: When is a good or service 'distinct' under IFRS 15?
options: When the customer can benefit from it on its own or with other readily available resources, and the promise to transfer it is separately identifiable
When it is the only item in the contract
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.correct: When the customer can benefit from it on its own or with other readily available resources, and the promise to transfer it is separately identifiable
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.explanation: A good or service is distinct if the customer can benefit from it on its own or with other readily available resources, and the entity's promise to transfer it is separately identifiable from other promises.

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.question: What is the transaction price under IFRS 15?
options: The amount of consideration to which the entity expects to be entitled in exchange for transferring goods or services
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.correct: The amount of consideration to which the entity expects to be entitled in exchange for transferring goods or services
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.explanation: The transaction price is the amount of consideration to which the entity expects to be entitled in exchange for transferring the promised goods or services, excluding amounts collected on behalf of third parties.

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.question: How should variable consideration be included in the transaction price under IFRS 15?
options: At the amount to which the entity expects to be entitled (constrained where necessary)
It is never included until received
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.correct: At the amount to which the entity expects to be entitled (constrained where necessary)
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.explanation: Variable consideration is estimated (using expected value or most likely amount) and included in the transaction price only to the extent that it is highly probable that a significant reversal will not occur.

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.question: When does control of a good transfer to the customer under IFRS 15?
options: When the customer has physical possession
When the customer has the ability to direct the use of and obtain substantially all remaining benefits from the asset
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.correct: When the customer has the ability to direct the use of and obtain substantially all remaining benefits from the asset
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.explanation: Control transfers when the customer has the ability to direct the use of and obtain substantially all of the remaining benefits from the asset. This may be over time or at a point in time.

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.question: What is a contract asset under IFRS 15?
options: An entity's right to consideration in exchange for goods or services transferred when that right is conditional on something other than the passage of time
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.correct: An entity's right to consideration in exchange for goods or services transferred when that right is conditional on something other than the passage of time
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.explanation: A contract asset is an entity's right to consideration that is conditional on something other than the passage of time (e.g. further performance). A receivable is unconditional (subject only to passage of time).

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.question: How does IFRS 15 treat contract costs?
options: Incremental costs of obtaining a contract and costs to fulfil that meet criteria are capitalised and amortised or impaired appropriately
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.correct: Incremental costs of obtaining a contract and costs to fulfil that meet criteria are capitalised and amortised or impaired appropriately
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.explanation: Incremental costs of obtaining a contract (e.g. sales commission) and certain costs to fulfil a contract are capitalised as an asset and amortised or tested for impairment.

Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.question: When may an entity recognise revenue over time under IFRS 15?
options: When one of the criteria is met: customer receives and consumes as performed; entity's work creates/enhances an asset the customer controls; or entity's work has no alternative use and entity has right to payment
Only when the contract is long-term
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.correct: When one of the criteria is met: customer receives and consumes as performed; entity's work creates/enhances an asset the customer controls; or entity's work has no alternative use and entity has right to payment
Chapter 5: IFRS 15 Revenue from Contract.explanation: Revenue is recognised over time if (a) the customer simultaneously receives and consumes as the entity performs, (b) the entity's work creates/enhances an asset the customer controls, or (c) the entity's work has no alternative use and the entity has an enforceable right to payment for performance to date.

Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.question: Under IAS 2, at what amount should inventories be measured?
options: Lower of cost and net realisable value
Lower of cost and replacement cost
Higher of cost and net realisable value
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.correct: Lower of cost and net realisable value
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.explanation: IAS 2 requires inventories to be measured at the lower of cost and net realisable value. Net realisable value is estimated selling price less costs to complete and sell.

Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.question: What costs may be included in the cost of inventories under IAS 2?
options: Purchase price, conversion costs, and other costs incurred in bringing inventories to their present location and condition
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.correct: Purchase price, conversion costs, and other costs incurred in bringing inventories to their present location and condition
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.explanation: Cost includes purchase price, conversion costs (direct labour, overheads) and other costs incurred in bringing inventories to their present location and condition. It excludes selling costs and abnormal waste.

Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.question: Which cost formula does IAS 2 permit for interchangeable items?
options: FIFO, weighted average, or specific identification when appropriate
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.correct: FIFO, weighted average, or specific identification when appropriate
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.explanation: IAS 2 permits FIFO or weighted average. Specific identification is used for items that are not interchangeable or produced for specific projects. LIFO is not permitted.

Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.question: What is net realisable value under IAS 2?
options: Estimated selling price in the ordinary course of business less estimated costs of completion and estimated costs to make the sale
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.correct: Estimated selling price in the ordinary course of business less estimated costs of completion and estimated costs to make the sale
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.explanation: NRV is the estimated selling price in the ordinary course of business less the estimated costs of completion and the estimated costs necessary to make the sale.

Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.question: How should a write-down of inventory to NRV be recognised under IAS 2?
options: As an expense in profit or loss
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.correct: As an expense in profit or loss
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.explanation: A write-down of inventory to NRV is recognised as an expense in profit or loss in the period of the write-down.

Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.question: Does IAS 2 apply to biological assets related to agricultural activity?
options: No; IAS 41 applies to biological assets, though IAS 2 may apply to agricultural produce at point of harvest
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.correct: No; IAS 41 applies to biological assets, though IAS 2 may apply to agricultural produce at point of harvest
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.explanation: IAS 41 applies to biological assets (living animals or plants). Agricultural produce harvested from a biological asset is measured under IAS 2 at point of harvest (at fair value less costs to sell at that point).

Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.question: What are conversion costs in the context of IAS 2?
options: Costs directly related to units of production (e.g. direct labour) and systematic allocation of fixed and variable production overheads
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.correct: Costs directly related to units of production (e.g. direct labour) and systematic allocation of fixed and variable production overheads
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.explanation: Conversion costs include direct labour and a systematic allocation of fixed and variable production overheads that are incurred in converting materials into finished goods.

Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.question: When may inventory be stated above cost under IAS 2?
options: Never; inventory is always at the lower of cost and NRV
When it is held by a broker
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.correct: Never; inventory is always at the lower of cost and NRV
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.explanation: Inventories are measured at the lower of cost and NRV. They are not stated above cost under IAS 2.

Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.question: How should inventory held by a broker-trader be measured under IAS 2?
options: At fair value less costs to sell
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.correct: At fair value less costs to sell
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.explanation: Commodity broker-traders may measure inventories at fair value less costs to sell when this is a well-established practice. Otherwise the lower of cost and NRV applies.

Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.question: What disclosure does IAS 2 require for inventories?
options: The accounting policy, carrying amount by category, amount recognised as expense, and any write-down or reversal
Only in the statement of cash flows
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.correct: The accounting policy, carrying amount by category, amount recognised as expense, and any write-down or reversal
Chapter 6: Inventories and Agriculture.explanation: IAS 2 requires disclosure of accounting policies, carrying amount by category, amount of inventories recognised as expense, write-downs and reversals, and other specified information.

Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.question: Under IAS 16, which of the following may be included in the cost of an item of property, plant and equipment?
options: Initial delivery and handling costs only
Costs of opening a new facility only
Directly attributable costs to bring the asset to working condition
Administrative overheads of the purchasing department
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.correct: Directly attributable costs to bring the asset to working condition
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.explanation: The cost of PPE includes its purchase price and any directly attributable costs to bring the asset to the location and condition necessary for it to be capable of operating in the manner intended by management.

Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.question: What measurement models does IAS 16 allow after initial recognition?
options: Fair value through profit or loss
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.explanation: After initial recognition, an entity chooses either the cost model (cost less accumulated depreciation and impairment) or the revaluation model (fair value at revaluation date less subsequent depreciation and impairment).

Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.question: How is depreciation defined under IAS 16?
options: Allocation of replacement cost over useful life
Systematic allocation of the depreciable amount of an asset over its useful life
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.correct: Systematic allocation of the depreciable amount of an asset over its useful life
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.explanation: Depreciation is the systematic allocation of the depreciable amount of an asset over its useful life. Depreciable amount is cost (or revalued amount) less residual value.

Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.question: What is the useful life of an asset under IAS 16?
options: The period the entity intends to use it
The period over which an asset is expected to be available for use by the entity, or the number of production units expected from it
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.correct: The period over which an asset is expected to be available for use by the entity, or the number of production units expected from it
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.explanation: Useful life is either the period over which the asset is expected to be available for use by the entity or the number of production units expected to be obtained from it.

Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.question: When must the depreciation method and useful life be reviewed under IAS 16?
options: At least at each financial year end
Only when there is a major repair
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.correct: At least at each financial year end
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.explanation: The depreciation method and useful life (and residual value) should be reviewed at least at each annual reporting date. If expectations differ from previous estimates, the change is accounted for as a change in estimate (prospective).

Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.question: How is a revaluation surplus on PPE treated under IAS 16?
options: In other comprehensive income and accumulated in equity under revaluation surplus
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.correct: In other comprehensive income and accumulated in equity under revaluation surplus
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.explanation: Increases in carrying amount on revaluation are recognised in other comprehensive income and accumulated in equity under revaluation surplus, except to the extent they reverse previous revaluation decreases of the same asset that were recognised in profit or loss.

Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.question: What happens when an item of PPE is revalued and the revaluation is a decrease?
options: Recognised in profit or loss to the extent it exceeds any previous revaluation surplus for that asset
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.correct: Recognised in profit or loss to the extent it exceeds any previous revaluation surplus for that asset
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.explanation: A revaluation decrease is recognised in profit or loss. However, to the extent that a revaluation surplus exists for that asset, the decrease is recognised in other comprehensive income (reducing the surplus).

Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.question: Can components of an item of PPE have different useful lives under IAS 16?
options: No; one useful life per item
Yes; each significant part with a cost that is significant in relation to the total cost of the item is depreciated separately
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.correct: Yes; each significant part with a cost that is significant in relation to the total cost of the item is depreciated separately
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.explanation: If parts of an item of PPE have different useful lives or provide benefits in a different pattern, each part is depreciated separately (e.g. airframe and engines of an aircraft).

Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.question: What is residual value under IAS 16?
options: The estimated amount that an entity would currently obtain from disposal of the asset, after deducting estimated disposal costs, if the asset were already of the age and condition expected at the end of its useful life
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.correct: The estimated amount that an entity would currently obtain from disposal of the asset, after deducting estimated disposal costs, if the asset were already of the age and condition expected at the end of its useful life
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.explanation: Residual value is the estimated amount (excluding disposal costs) that could be obtained from disposal of the asset at the end of its useful life, in its expected condition.

Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.question: When may depreciation cease under IAS 16?
options: When the asset is fully depreciated or when it is classified as held for sale
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.correct: When the asset is fully depreciated or when it is classified as held for sale
Chapter 7: IAS 16 Property, Plant and Eq.explanation: Depreciation continues until the asset is derecognised or classified as held for sale. Depreciation does not cease when the asset is idle or retired from active use unless it is fully depreciated or held for sale.

Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.question: Under IAS 23, when must borrowing costs be capitalised?
options: When the loan is used for any purpose
When the borrowing is directly attributable to a qualifying asset
Only when the asset is inventory
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.correct: When the borrowing is directly attributable to a qualifying asset
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.explanation: IAS 23 requires capitalisation of borrowing costs that are directly attributable to the acquisition, construction or production of a qualifying asset. A qualifying asset is one that necessarily takes a substantial period to get ready for use or sale.

Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.question: What is a qualifying asset under IAS 23?
options: An asset that necessarily takes a substantial period of time to get ready for its intended use or sale
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.correct: An asset that necessarily takes a substantial period of time to get ready for its intended use or sale
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.explanation: A qualifying asset is one that necessarily takes a substantial period of time to get ready for its intended use or sale (e.g. manufacturing plant, power generation facility, investment property).

Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.question: Which borrowing costs are capitalised under IAS 23?
options: Borrowing costs that are directly attributable to the acquisition, construction or production of a qualifying asset
Only costs incurred after construction starts
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.correct: Borrowing costs that are directly attributable to the acquisition, construction or production of a qualifying asset
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.explanation: Only borrowing costs that are directly attributable to the acquisition, construction or production of a qualifying asset are capitalised. Other borrowing costs are expensed.

Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.question: How is the capitalisation of borrowing costs commenced under IAS 23?
options: When expenditure on the asset, borrowing costs and activities necessary to prepare the asset for use or sale have begun
When the asset is ready for use
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.correct: When expenditure on the asset, borrowing costs and activities necessary to prepare the asset for use or sale have begun
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.explanation: Capitalisation begins when (a) expenditure on the asset is being incurred, (b) borrowing costs are being incurred, and (c) activities necessary to prepare the asset for use or sale are in progress.

Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.question: When does capitalisation of borrowing costs cease under IAS 23?
options: When substantially all the activities necessary to prepare the qualifying asset for its intended use or sale are complete
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.correct: When substantially all the activities necessary to prepare the qualifying asset for its intended use or sale are complete
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.explanation: Capitalisation ceases when substantially all the activities necessary to prepare the qualifying asset for its intended use or sale are complete (e.g. physical construction is complete, even if minor modifications remain).

Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.question: If funds are borrowed specifically for a qualifying asset, how are capitalisable borrowing costs determined?
options: At the entity's weighted average borrowing rate
At the actual borrowing costs incurred on that borrowing during the period, less any investment income on temporary investment of those borrowings
At the interest rate in the loan agreement only
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.correct: At the actual borrowing costs incurred on that borrowing during the period, less any investment income on temporary investment of those borrowings
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.explanation: When borrowings are made specifically for a qualifying asset, the borrowing costs eligible for capitalisation are the actual costs incurred less any investment income on the temporary investment of those borrowings.

Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.question: When general borrowings are used for a qualifying asset, how is the capitalisation rate determined under IAS 23?
options: The rate on the most recent loan
The weighted average of the borrowing costs applicable to general borrowings outstanding during the period
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.correct: The weighted average of the borrowing costs applicable to general borrowings outstanding during the period
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.explanation: When funds are borrowed generally and used for a qualifying asset, the entity uses the weighted average of borrowing costs applicable to borrowings outstanding during the period (excluding specific borrowings).

Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.question: Can borrowing costs be capitalised for inventory that is routinely manufactured or produced in large quantities?
options: No; IAS 23 does not require capitalisation for such inventory
Only if the inventory is held for more than one year
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.correct: No; IAS 23 does not require capitalisation for such inventory
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.explanation: IAS 23 does not require capitalisation of borrowing costs for inventory that is manufactured or produced in large quantities on a repetitive basis (e.g. maturing whisky).

Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.question: Where is capitalised borrowing cost recognised under IAS 23?
options: As part of the cost of the qualifying asset
As a reduction in the related borrowing
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.correct: As part of the cost of the qualifying asset
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.explanation: Borrowing costs capitalised under IAS 23 are added to the carrying amount of the qualifying asset. They are then depreciated or expensed (e.g. when inventory is sold) as part of that asset.

Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.question: What disclosure does IAS 23 require for borrowing costs?
options: The amount of borrowing costs capitalised during the period and the capitalisation rate used
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.correct: The amount of borrowing costs capitalised during the period and the capitalisation rate used
Chapter 8: IAS 23 Borrowing Costs.explanation: IAS 23 requires disclosure of the amount of borrowing costs capitalised during the period and the capitalisation rate(s) used to determine the amount eligible for capitalisation.

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: Under IAS 20, how may a government grant related to an asset be presented in the statement of financial position?
options: Only as a deduction from the carrying amount of the asset
Either as a deduction from the asset or as deferred income
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: Either as a deduction from the asset or as deferred income
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: IAS 20 permits two approaches: presenting the grant as deferred income (and releasing to profit or loss over the asset's life) or deducting it from the carrying amount of the asset.

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: When should a government grant be recognised under IAS 20?
options: When there is reasonable assurance that the entity will comply with conditions attaching to the grant and the grant will be received
When the related expenditure is incurred
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: When there is reasonable assurance that the entity will comply with conditions attaching to the grant and the grant will be received
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: A government grant is recognised when there is reasonable assurance that the entity will comply with the conditions attaching to it and that the grant will be received.

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: How should a grant related to income be recognised under IAS 20?
options: As income over the periods necessary to match them with the related costs they are intended to compensate
As a liability until conditions are met
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: As income over the periods necessary to match them with the related costs they are intended to compensate
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: Grants related to income (e.g. to subsidise operating expenses) are recognised in profit or loss over the periods in which the related costs are recognised.

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: If a grant related to an asset is presented as deferred income, how is it released to profit or loss?
options: In full when the asset is acquired
Systematically over the useful life of the asset (e.g. in line with depreciation)
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: Systematically over the useful life of the asset (e.g. in line with depreciation)
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: When the grant is presented as deferred income, it is recognised in profit or loss over the useful life of the related asset (e.g. in the same proportion as depreciation).

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: What happens if an entity fails to comply with conditions attaching to a government grant?
options: The grant or the relevant part is repaid; any amount already recognised as income may need to be reversed
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: The grant or the relevant part is repaid; any amount already recognised as income may need to be reversed
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: If the entity does not comply with conditions, the grant may become repayable. Repayment is accounted for by revising the deferred income/asset and recognising the repayment as an expense (or reducing the asset).

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: Can government grants include non-monetary assets under IAS 20?
options: Yes; they are usually recognised at fair value or at a nominal amount
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: Yes; they are usually recognised at fair value or at a nominal amount
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: Government grants may be in the form of non-monetary assets (e.g. land, equipment). These are usually recognised at fair value or at a nominal amount, with the grant then accounted for in the same way as monetary grants.

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: How should repayment of a grant related to an asset previously presented as a deduction from the asset be accounted for?
options: By increasing the carrying amount of the asset or recognising a loss to the extent the repayment exceeds any related deferred credit
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: By increasing the carrying amount of the asset or recognising a loss to the extent the repayment exceeds any related deferred credit
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: Repayment of a grant related to an asset is accounted for by increasing the carrying amount of the asset or recognising a loss to the extent that the repayment exceeds any deferred credit (or the grant had been presented as a deduction from the asset).

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: What disclosure does IAS 20 require for government grants?
options: The accounting policy, the nature and extent of grants recognised, and unfulfilled conditions or other contingencies
Only the amount received in the period
Only in the cash flow statement
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: The accounting policy, the nature and extent of grants recognised, and unfulfilled conditions or other contingencies
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: IAS 20 requires disclosure of the accounting policy adopted, the nature and extent of government grants recognised in the financial statements, and unfulfilled conditions or other contingencies attaching to grants.

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: Is there a single mandatory method of presenting grants related to assets under IAS 20?
options: No; either presentation (deduction from asset or deferred income) is permitted
Grants related to assets are not covered
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: No; either presentation (deduction from asset or deferred income) is permitted
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: IAS 20 permits two approaches: (1) setting up the grant as deferred income and releasing to profit or loss over the asset's life, or (2) deducting the grant from the carrying amount of the asset.

Chapter 9: Government Grants.question: When might a grant not be recognised as income under IAS 20?
options: When there is no reasonable assurance of compliance or receipt
When the grant is in kind
Chapter 9: Government Grants.correct: When there is no reasonable assurance of compliance or receipt
Chapter 9: Government Grants.explanation: A grant is recognised only when there is reasonable assurance that the entity will comply with the conditions and that the grant will be received. If that assurance is lacking, the grant is not recognised until the uncertainty is resolved.

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: Under IAS 40, which measurement model(s) may an entity use for investment property after initial recognition?
options: Either cost model or fair value model
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: Either cost model or fair value model
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: IAS 40 allows an entity to choose either the cost model or the fair value model for all investment property. The choice is applied consistently to all such property.

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: What is investment property under IAS 40?
options: Property used in the production of goods or for administration
Property held to earn rentals or for capital appreciation or both, rather than for use in production or supply or for sale in the ordinary course of business
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: Property held to earn rentals or for capital appreciation or both, rather than for use in production or supply or for sale in the ordinary course of business
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: Investment property is property (land or building, or part of a building) held to earn rentals or for capital appreciation or both, not for use in production/supply or for sale in the ordinary course of business.

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: Is property held for sale in the ordinary course of business investment property?
options: No; it is inventory under IAS 2
Only if it is a building
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: No; it is inventory under IAS 2
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: Property held for sale in the ordinary course of business (e.g. by a property developer) is inventory under IAS 2, not investment property.

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: Under the fair value model in IAS 40, where are changes in fair value recognised?
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: Under the fair value model, changes in fair value of investment property are recognised in profit or loss in the period in which they arise.

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: When can an entity not determine fair value reliably for investment property under IAS 40?
options: Never; fair value can always be determined
In exceptional cases only when the entity cannot determine fair value reliably on a continuing basis; then the cost model is used
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: In exceptional cases only when the entity cannot determine fair value reliably on a continuing basis; then the cost model is used
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: If an entity cannot determine fair value reliably on a continuing basis, it uses the cost model under IAS 16. This is expected to be rare (e.g. for unique property).

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: Can a property be partly investment property and partly owner-occupied under IAS 40?
options: Yes; if the portions can be sold or leased separately, they are accounted for separately
Only if they are in different countries
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: Yes; if the portions can be sold or leased separately, they are accounted for separately
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: If the portions could be sold or leased separately, they are accounted for separately (e.g. one part investment property, one part owner-occupied). If not, the property is investment property only if an insignificant portion is owner-occupied.

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: How is investment property initially measured under IAS 40?
options: At cost, including directly attributable expenditure
At the lower of cost and fair value
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: At cost, including directly attributable expenditure
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: Investment property is initially measured at cost, including transaction costs. Cost is determined in accordance with the same principles as for PPE (e.g. IAS 16).

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: When does a transfer from inventory to investment property occur under IAS 40?
options: When management decides to hold for rental
When there is a change in use, with evidence of the change (e.g. commencement of an operating lease to a third party)
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: When there is a change in use, with evidence of the change (e.g. commencement of an operating lease to a third party)
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: A transfer from inventory to investment property is made when there is a change in use, evidenced by commencement of an operating lease to a third party. The property is reclassified and measured at fair value at the date of change.

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: If an entity uses the cost model for investment property, what standard applies for measurement?
options: IAS 16 applies (cost less accumulated depreciation and impairment)
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: IAS 16 applies (cost less accumulated depreciation and impairment)
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: Under the cost model, investment property is measured in accordance with IAS 16: cost less accumulated depreciation and accumulated impairment losses.

Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.question: What disclosure does IAS 40 require when the fair value model is used?
options: Whether the entity uses the cost or fair value model; methods and assumptions for determining fair value; and a reconciliation of carrying amounts
Only the fair value at the reporting date
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.correct: Whether the entity uses the cost or fair value model; methods and assumptions for determining fair value; and a reconciliation of carrying amounts
Chapter 10: IAS 40 Investment Property.explanation: IAS 40 requires disclosure of whether cost or fair value is used, and when fair value is used: the methods and significant assumptions; and a reconciliation of the opening to closing carrying amount of investment property.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: Under IAS 38, when may development costs be capitalised as an intangible asset?
options: Whenever management intends to use the asset
Only when all six recognition criteria are met
When the project is technically feasible only
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: Only when all six recognition criteria are met
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: Development costs can be capitalised only when the entity can demonstrate technical feasibility, intention and ability to complete and use or sell the asset, and that the asset will generate probable future economic benefits, among other criteria.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: How are research costs treated under IAS 38?
options: Capitalised when the project is approved
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: Research (or the research phase of an internal project) is expensed when incurred. No intangible asset arising from research (or from the research phase) is recognised.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: What is an intangible asset under IAS 38?
options: Any non-monetary asset without physical substance
An identifiable non-monetary asset without physical substance
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: An identifiable non-monetary asset without physical substance
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: An intangible asset is an identifiable non-monetary asset without physical substance. Identifiable means separable or arising from contractual or other legal rights.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: How is the useful life of an intangible asset determined under IAS 38?
options: As finite or indefinite; finite life is the period over which the asset is expected to provide benefits
Management estimate only with no ceiling
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: As finite or indefinite; finite life is the period over which the asset is expected to provide benefits
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: An intangible asset has either a finite or an indefinite useful life. A finite life is the length of time the asset is expected to be available for use by the entity.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: How is an intangible asset with an indefinite useful life measured after initial recognition under IAS 38?
options: At cost (or revalued amount) less accumulated impairment losses; it is not amortised
At cost and amortised over 10 years
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: At cost (or revalued amount) less accumulated impairment losses; it is not amortised
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: An intangible asset with an indefinite useful life is not amortised. It is tested for impairment annually (and whenever there is an indication of impairment) and carried at cost less accumulated impairment losses.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: Can intangible assets be revalued under IAS 38?
options: Yes; only if there is an active market for the asset
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: Yes; only if there is an active market for the asset
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: After initial recognition, an intangible asset may be carried at a revalued amount (fair value at revaluation date less subsequent amortisation and impairment) only if fair value can be determined by reference to an active market. Active markets for intangibles are rare.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: What is the default amortisation period for an intangible asset with a finite life when the useful life cannot be determined reliably?
options: The period is presumed to be 10 years if it cannot be determined reliably
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: IAS 38 states that the useful life of an intangible asset that is not amortised (i.e. indefinite) is reviewed each period. For finite-lived intangibles, if the useful life cannot be estimated reliably, it is presumed not to exceed 10 years in some jurisdictions; the standard requires the best estimate.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: How is goodwill arising in a business combination treated under IAS 38?
options: As an intangible asset under IAS 38
It is not covered by IAS 38; IFRS 3 applies to goodwill acquired in a business combination
As a separate asset under IAS 38
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: It is not covered by IAS 38; IFRS 3 applies to goodwill acquired in a business combination
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: Goodwill acquired in a business combination is outside the scope of IAS 38. It is accounted for under IFRS 3 (not amortised; tested for impairment annually).

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: When may an entity derecognise an intangible asset under IAS 38?
options: When it is fully amortised only
When it is disposed of or when no future economic benefits are expected from its use or disposal
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: When it is disposed of or when no future economic benefits are expected from its use or disposal
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: An intangible asset is derecognised on disposal or when no future economic benefits are expected from its use or disposal. The gain or loss is the difference between net disposal proceeds and carrying amount.

Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.question: What disclosure does IAS 38 require for intangible assets?
options: Accounting policies; useful lives or amortisation rates; reconciliation of carrying amount; and restrictions on title
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.correct: Accounting policies; useful lives or amortisation rates; reconciliation of carrying amount; and restrictions on title
Chapter 11: IAS 38 Intangible Assets.explanation: IAS 38 requires disclosure of accounting policies, useful lives/amortisation rates, a reconciliation of the opening to closing carrying amount, and restrictions on title or pledged as security, among other items.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: Under IFRS 5, how should a non-current asset held for sale be measured?
options: At the lower of carrying amount and fair value less costs to sell
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: At the lower of carrying amount and fair value less costs to sell
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: IFRS 5 requires non-current assets (or disposal groups) classified as held for sale to be measured at the lower of carrying amount and fair value less costs to sell. Depreciation ceases.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: When can a non-current asset (or disposal group) be classified as held for sale under IFRS 5?
options: When management is considering a sale
When it is available for immediate sale in its present condition and the sale is highly probable
When a buyer has been found
When the asset is no longer used
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: When it is available for immediate sale in its present condition and the sale is highly probable
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: Classification as held for sale requires that the asset (or disposal group) is available for immediate sale in its present condition and that the sale is highly probable (e.g. committed to a plan, active programme to locate a buyer, expected within one year).

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: What happens to depreciation when an asset is classified as held for sale?
options: It is charged only in the period of classification
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: When a non-current asset (or disposal group) is classified as held for sale, the entity does not depreciate (or amortise) the asset while it is so classified.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: What is a discontinued operation under IFRS 5?
options: A component of an entity that has been disposed of or is classified as held for sale and represents a separate major line of business or geographical area, or is a subsidiary acquired exclusively with a view to resale
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: A component of an entity that has been disposed of or is classified as held for sale and represents a separate major line of business or geographical area, or is a subsidiary acquired exclusively with a view to resale
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: A discontinued operation is a component of an entity that has been disposed of or is classified as held for sale and (a) represents a separate major line of business or geographical area, or (b) is part of a single coordinated plan to dispose of a separate major line of business or geographical area, or (c) is a subsidiary acquired exclusively with a view to resale.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: How are the results of a discontinued operation presented in the statement of profit or loss under IFRS 5?
options: As a single line item only in the notes
As a single amount in profit or loss, with additional disclosure of revenue, expenses and pre-tax profit or loss of the discontinued operation
Only in the period of disposal
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: As a single amount in profit or loss, with additional disclosure of revenue, expenses and pre-tax profit or loss of the discontinued operation
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: The post-tax profit or loss of the discontinued operation and the post-tax gain or loss on disposal are presented as a single amount in the statement of profit or loss, with disclosure of revenue, expenses and pre-tax profit or loss of the discontinued operation.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: Can a disposal group include current assets under IFRS 5?
options: Yes; a disposal group may include assets and liabilities (including current assets and current liabilities) to be disposed of together
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: Yes; a disposal group may include assets and liabilities (including current assets and current liabilities) to be disposed of together
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: A disposal group is a group of assets to be disposed of, together as a group in a single transaction, and possibly liabilities directly associated with those assets. It can include current and non-current assets and liabilities.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: If the criteria for held for sale are no longer met, how should the asset (or disposal group) be accounted for under IFRS 5?
options: It is reclassified back to the appropriate standard (e.g. IAS 16) and measured at the lower of (a) its carrying amount before classification as held for sale (adjusted for depreciation that would have been recognised) and (b) its recoverable amount at the date of the decision not to sell
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: It is reclassified back to the appropriate standard (e.g. IAS 16) and measured at the lower of (a) its carrying amount before classification as held for sale (adjusted for depreciation that would have been recognised) and (b) its recoverable amount at the date of the decision not to sell
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: When the criteria for held for sale are no longer met, the asset (or disposal group) is reclassified. It is measured at the lower of (a) its carrying amount before classification as held for sale (adjusted for depreciation/amortisation that would have been recognised) and (b) its recoverable amount at the date of the decision not to sell.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: What are 'costs to sell' under IFRS 5?
options: Incremental costs directly attributable to the disposal, excluding finance costs and income tax expense
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: Incremental costs directly attributable to the disposal, excluding finance costs and income tax expense
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: Costs to sell are the incremental costs directly attributable to the disposal of an asset (or disposal group), excluding finance costs and income tax expense.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: When an asset is reclassified from held for sale, how is the recovery in value (above the adjusted carrying amount) treated?
options: The asset is measured at the lower of the adjusted carrying amount and recoverable amount; any increase above the pre-classification carrying amount is limited by what depreciation would have been
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: The asset is measured at the lower of the adjusted carrying amount and recoverable amount; any increase above the pre-classification carrying amount is limited by what depreciation would have been
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: On reclassification, the asset is measured at the lower of (a) its carrying amount before classification as held for sale (adjusted for depreciation that would have been recognised) and (b) recoverable amount at the date of the decision not to sell. The standard does not permit carrying amount to exceed what it would have been without the held-for-sale classification.

Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.question: Is a non-current asset that is to be abandoned (e.g. no sale) classified as held for sale?
options: No; assets to be abandoned are not classified as held for sale because they will not be recovered principally through a sale transaction
Only if abandonment is within one year
Only if it is a disposal group
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.correct: No; assets to be abandoned are not classified as held for sale because they will not be recovered principally through a sale transaction
Chapter 12: IFRS 5 Non-current Assets He.explanation: Assets (or disposal groups) to be abandoned are not classified as held for sale. Abandonment means the asset will be used until the end of its useful life or will be exchanged for another asset; recovery is not principally through a sale.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: Under IAS 36, when must an entity perform an impairment test for an asset?
options: When there is an indication of impairment, or annually for goodwill and certain intangibles
Only when the asset's market value falls below cost
Never for assets held at historical cost
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.correct: When there is an indication of impairment, or annually for goodwill and certain intangibles
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: An impairment test is required when there are indicators of impairment. In addition, goodwill and indefinite-life intangibles (and certain other intangibles not yet available for use) must be tested for impairment annually.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: What is recoverable amount under IAS 36?
options: The higher of an asset's fair value less costs of disposal and its value in use
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.correct: The higher of an asset's fair value less costs of disposal and its value in use
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: Recoverable amount is the higher of an asset's fair value less costs of disposal and its value in use.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: What is value in use under IAS 36?
options: The present value of the future cash flows expected to be derived from an asset (or cash-generating unit)
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.correct: The present value of the future cash flows expected to be derived from an asset (or cash-generating unit)
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: Value in use is the present value of the future cash flows expected to be derived from an asset or cash-generating unit. It includes estimated future cash inflows and outflows and the appropriate discount rate.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: How is an impairment loss recognised for an individual asset under IAS 36?
options: In profit or loss, and the carrying amount of the asset is reduced (or the related revaluation surplus is reduced first if applicable)
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.correct: In profit or loss, and the carrying amount of the asset is reduced (or the related revaluation surplus is reduced first if applicable)
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: An impairment loss is recognised in profit or loss. The carrying amount of the asset is reduced. For a revalued asset, the loss is recognised in OCI to the extent of any revaluation surplus for that asset; the remainder in profit or loss.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: What is a cash-generating unit under IAS 36?
options: The smallest identifiable group of assets that generates cash inflows that are largely independent of the cash inflows from other assets or groups of assets
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.correct: The smallest identifiable group of assets that generates cash inflows that are largely independent of the cash inflows from other assets or groups of assets
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: A cash-generating unit is the smallest identifiable group of assets that generates cash inflows that are largely independent of the cash inflows from other assets or groups of assets.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: How is goodwill allocated for impairment testing under IAS 36?
options: To the entity as a whole

To each cash-generating unit (or group of units) that is expected to benefit from the synergies of the combination; the allocation is not larger than the level at which goodwill is monitored internally
Goodwill is not tested for impairment
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.correct: To each cash-generating unit (or group of units) that is expected to benefit from the synergies of the combination; the allocation is not larger than the level at which goodwill is monitored internally
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: Goodwill acquired in a business combination is allocated to each of the acquirer's cash-generating units (or groups of units) that are expected to benefit from the synergies. The allocation is at a level not larger than an operating segment.

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: Can an impairment loss be reversed for goodwill under IAS 36?
options: No; an impairment loss recognised for goodwill is not reversed in a subsequent period
Only if the reason for the loss no longer applies
Only in the year after the loss
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.correct: No; an impairment loss recognised for goodwill is not reversed in a subsequent period
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: An impairment loss recognised for goodwill is not reversed in a subsequent period. For other assets, reversal is permitted in certain circumstances (recoverable amount increases; reversal is limited).

Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.question: When can an impairment loss (other than for goodwill) be reversed under IAS 36?
options: When there has been a change in the estimates used to determine recoverable amount; the increased carrying amount cannot exceed what the carrying amount would have been (net of depreciation/amortisation) had no impairment been recognised
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.correct: When there has been a change in the estimates used to determine recoverable amount; the increased carrying amount cannot exceed what the carrying amount would have been (net of depreciation/amortisation) had no impairment been recognised
Chapter 13: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets.explanation: A reversal of an impairment loss (for assets other than goodwill) is recognised when there has been a change in the estimates used.
